Aryna Sabalenka describes the tennis schedule as “insane” and said she'll risk fines in order to skip tournaments to avoid injuries or burnout.
For all those concerns, though, the world's top-ranked woman is happy to have as much competition as possible before the season-opening Grand Slam in Australia.
Sabalenka beat Sorana Cirstea 6-3, 6-3 on Thursday to move into the Brisbane International quarterfinals, where she'll take on fifth-seeded Madison Keys almost a year after losing to her in the Australian Open final.
Keys, her left leg heavily taped, came back from a set and a break down to beat 12th-seeded Diana Shnaider 6-7 (5), 7-6 (5), 7-6 (4) on Thursday.
“Always good to start the year with a little bit of drama,” Keys said in a post-match interview after three hours on court. “Glad we got that out of the way.”
Keys had only two matches after her first-round exit at the U.S. Open, so she’s looking for plenty of time on court before her title defense at the Australian Open, which starts on Jan. 18. Her career breakthrough at a major came at the expense of Sabalenka's bid for an Australian Open three-peat.
Keys got more time on court in Brisbane than she bargained for in three tiebreakers, a medical timeout to have her upper left leg taped after the second tiebreaker, and a third set lasting 69 minutes.
The American missed two match points in the 10th game of the third set but made sure of it in the deciding tiebreaker. It continued her winning streak in Australia that includes titles in Adelaide and at Melbourne Park in 2025.
“It’s always a little bit nerve-wracking

“It’s going to be, as always, a great battle, really aggressive tennis,” she said. “I’m excited to face her.”

Israel says Nickolay Mladenov to direct Trump’s proposed Gaza ‘peace board’
Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu says that former United Nations Middle East envoy Nickolay Mladenov will direct a proposed United States-led “board of peace” in Gaza. Netanyahu made the announcement after meeting Mladenov in Jerusalem on Thursday, referring to the Bulgarian diplomat as the “designated” director-general for the proposed board, a key part of US President Donald Trump’s 20-point plan to end Israel’s genocidal war on the Palestinian people of Gaza. Recommended Stories list of 3 items list 1 of 3Lebanon army says phase one of disarming non-state groups in south complete list 2 of 3Top Somaliland official defends Israel ties amid Arab backlash list 3 of 3Iran experiencing nationwide internet blackout, monitor says end of list Trump’s Gaza plan led to a tenuous ceasefire between Israel and Hamas in October, but Israeli forces have continued to carry out attacks in the territory on a near-daily basis. Since the first full day of the truce on October 11, 2025, Israeli attacks have killed at least 425 Palestinians, according to the Gaza Health Ministry. In a statement on Thursday, Netanyahu’s office said Mladenov “is slated to serve as Director General of the ‘Peace Council’ in the Gaza Strip”. Israel’s President Isaac Herzog also met Mladenov on Thursday, a spokesperson from his office said, without elaborating. Under Trump’s plan to end the war, the proposed Board of Peace would supervise a new technocratic Palestinians government, the disarmament of Hamas, the deployment of an international security force, the further pushback of Israeli troops, and the reconstruction of the war-ravaged Gaza Strip. Trump is expected to announce appointments to the board next week, according to the Axios news outlet, citing US officials and sources familiar with the matter. “Among the countries expected to join the board are the UK, Germany, France, Italy, Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Egypt and Turkiye,” Axios reported. Mladenov, a former Bulgarian defence and foreign minister, previously served as the UN envoy to Iraq before being appointed as the UN Middle East peace envoy from 2015 to 2020. During his time as Middle East envoy, Mladenov had good working relations with Israel and frequently worked to ease tensions between Israel and Hamas.

### 923 Can a dynastic heir lead a post-dynasty Bangladesh?
On Christmas Day this year, Tarique Rahman – the heir apparent of the Bangladesh Nationalist Party (BNP) and the man many believe could be the country’s next prime minister – returned home and stepped directly into a power vacuum that has been steadily widening since the collapse of the Awami League government in August 2024. After 17 years in exile, Rahman’s act of touching the soil was carefully staged for the cameras, but its consequences are structural rather than symbolic. Bangladesh today is a state without a steady pulse, and his return has brought the country’s brief post-revolutionary interlude to an end. Five days later, on December 30, the political moment hardened into historical finality. Khaleda Zia – the former prime minister and wife of BNP founder and former Bangladesh President Ziaur Rahman – died after a prolonged illness, severing the last living link to the party’s original leadership generation. Rahman is no longer Khaleda Zia’s successor. He is now the leader of the BNP as it heads towards elections on February 12. The nation Rahman left in 2008 was fractured; the one he inhabits now is structurally compromised. The hurried flight of Sheikh Hasina to India after the uprising against her ended a decade and a half of autocratic rule but left behind a hollowed-out bureaucracy and a social contract in shreds. While Muhammad Yunus’s interim administration attempts to manage the transition, street power has already begun to bypass formal authority. In this volatility, Rahman’s presence acts as a high-voltage conductor for the BNP, providing a focal point for an opposition that was, until recently, systematically suppressed. For millions who viewed the last decade of elections under Hasina’s authoritarian grip as foregone conclusions, Rahman represents the return of choice. Yet Rahman is no insurgent outsider; he is the ultimate product of the system he seeks to lead. As the son of two former leaders of the country, he carries the weight of a dynastic legacy closely associated with the patronage networks that have long hobbled Bangladeshi governance. His earlier proximity to power was marked by allegations of informal authority and corruption ...

### 913 Top Somaliland official defends Israel ties amid Arab backlash
A senior official from Somaliland’s governing party has fiercely defended the breakaway region’s decision to normalise relations with Israel, dismissing widespread condemnation from the Arab and Muslim world as hypocritical. In a heated interview with Al Jazeera Mubasher on Wednesday, Hersi Ali Haji Hassan, chairman of the ruling Waddani party, argued that Somaliland was forced to look to Israel for legitimacy after being ignored by the international community for decades. The comments follow a controversial visit by Israeli Foreign Minister Gideon Saar to Hargeisa, the region’s largest city and “capital”, earlier this week – the first since Israel recognised the region’s independence in late December. “We are not in a position to choose,” Hassan told Al Jazeera. “We are in a state of necessity for official international recognition. “There is no choice before us but to welcome any country that recognises our existential right,” he added. ‘They ignored us for 34 years’ The visit has triggered a diplomatic firestorm. The federal government in Mogadishu, which considers Somaliland part of its territory, condemned the move as a violation of sovereignty. The Arab League and the Organisation of Islamic Cooperation (OIC) also issued statements rejecting the “separatist reality”. Hassan, however, brushed off the criticism. “We have been an independent state for 34 years,” he said. “The rejection of the Arab League does not matter to us at all. They did not accept us as a member … and we did not receive any attention from Arab countries.” When pressed on why Somaliland would ally with Israel while it is isolated in the region, Hassan pointed to other Arab nations. “Normalisation with Israel is not limited to Somaliland,” he argued. “Many Arab and Islamic countries have broad political and economic relations with Israel, such as Egypt, Turkiye, Jordan, and the UAE.” The military base question Regional powers are concerned that the new alliance could lead to an Israeli military foothold at the southern entrance to the Red Sea. When asked repeatedly if Somaliland would allow Israel to establish a military base on its soil, Hassan refused to rule it out explicitly. “We have started diplomatic relations… This topic [a military base] has not been touched upon now,” Hassan said. Pressed further on whether Hargeisa would accept such a request in the future, he replied: ...

blocks.content:
Resolution events:
- Bangladesh T20 venue resolved to Sri Lanka. We had Sri Lanka at 0.62, a useful example where host-structure evidence was enough even before a direct request article.
- Venezuela interim president resolved to Delcy Rodriguez. We included her only at 0.05 and overweighted Edmundo Gonzalez Urrutia; lesson is to keep constitutional/inside-regime continuity candidates higher when the question asks who is sworn in, not who outsiders recognise.

Updates made:
- Yemen: Al Jazeera reported that Yemen's Saudi-backed government appointed Salem al-Khanbashi to take overall command of the National Shield forces in Hadramout. Raised National Shield Forces for the anti-Houthi integration body and kept small PLC/Joint Operations/NRF backups.
- Morocco AFCON penalty: Straits Times/AFP reported Achraf Hakimi has barely featured while recovering from injury and Ayoub El Kaabi has become Morocco's tournament hero. Trimmed Hakimi/Brahim slightly and raised El Kaabi, while retaining Hakimi as the single top penalty-taker candidate.
- Iran protests: Jan. 2 reporting concentrated protest deaths and a state-TV-cited Basij death around Lorestan and Chaharmahal and Bakhtiari. Moved the province forecast from the older Sistan/Kurdistan baseline toward Lorestan, with Sistan and Baluchestan still a major backup.
- Starlink: no direct free-service article appeared, but Jan. 2 added two relevant contexts: Uganda election internet-shutdown fears with Starlink import restrictions, and Iran protests where outside internet access could become salient. Added Iran and raised Uganda while keeping Ukraine first and Venezuela live.
- Ukraine adviser: Zelenskyy named Kyrylo Budanov as head of the presidential office. This is not an economic-development-adviser role, but it is a near-date Zelenskyy appointment; added Budanov defensively while keeping Kubrakov/Jaresko/Svyrydenko as economic-policy candidates.
- Morocco calf injury: no calf-specific confirmation surfaced, but Hakimi remains the most prominent injured Morocco player in fresh AFCON coverage. Added him as a modest backup behind Romain Saiss.

Watched but no update:
- Greenland/Rubio/State Department, NATO Arctic mission naming, Gaza technocratic body/Board of Peace, Somalia severed agreements, Man United manager, Thailand NIDA polling, and near-term immigration-enforcement questions had no direct Jan. 2 evidence strong enough to move.
- The Budanov appointment is more relevant to political/security coordination than economics, so do not over-read it if new adviser-specific reporting appears.

## 2026-01-03 Update

Resolution events:
- Morocco calf injury resolved to Azzedine Ounahi. We missed him entirely after anchoring too much on visible injury coverage around Saiss/Hakimi. Lesson: for team-injury markets, include core midfielders/regular starters even when the article evidence is sparse.
- Free Starlink service resolved to Venezuela. We had Venezuela at 0.14 on Jan. 2 after reducing it from 0.18; the indirect Iran/Uganda additions were overfitted. Lesson: when a country is already tied to a live political/security disruption and the market wording fits it, do not dilute it without a direct counter-signal.

Updates made:
- Ukraine adviser/reshuffle: Straits Times reported Zelenskyy proposed Denys Shmyhal as energy minister and first deputy prime minister, praising his role in economic stability and energy-sector resilience. Raised Shmyhal for the near-term Zelenskyy appointment market, while keeping Kubrakov/Budanov/Svyrydenko/Jaresko as lower backups.
- 2026 World Cup host exclusion: the US attack on Venezuela triggered UK political criticism, and the question concerns a host country MPs may urge sporting authorities to exclude. Raised the United States substantially; Mexico and Canada remain small host-country backups.
- Grok ban: Jan. 3 reporting on Grok-generated sexualized images named French authorities, India's IT ministry, and a UK minister as government actors scrutinising the tool. Kept Turkey first from prior evidence but added France, India, and the United Kingdom.

Watched but no update:
- Syria/Aleppo, Maskana/Deir Hafer, Iran satellite-imagery site, Greenland talks/venue, AFCON DR Congo elimination, Qatar Board of Peace, immigration-enforcement states/agencies, Thailand NIDA, and Brisbane/Sabalenka produced no direct Jan. 3 signal.
- Venezuela oil/armada questions were affected by the large US operation, but no Thomas Massie/oil-proceeds country or exact "big armada" region wording appeared, so left those forecasts unchanged for now.

## 2026-01-04 Update

Resolution events:
- Ukraine economic development adviser resolved to Chrystia Freeland. We missed her entirely by focusing on internal Ukrainian reshuffle candidates. Lesson: when a question says "outside expertise", include internationally prominent allied economic-policy figures, especially Canadian/European former finance ministers.

Updates made:
- Syria/Aleppo: Al Jazeera reported Syrian government talks with the Kurdish-led SDF failed to make progress on military integration, while UK/France struck an ISIL site near Palmyra and related coverage noted Aleppo security violence. Raised SDF and consolidated more probability into ISIL wording rather than splitting too much into "Islamic State".
- AFCON DR Congo: tournament schedules now show Algeria v DR Congo as the key last-16 fixture. Raised Algeria and kept Nigeria as the main later-round backup if DR Congo upset Algeria.
- Grok ban: Straits Times/Bloomberg reported Malaysia, France, and India government action or threats over Grok-generated sexualised images. Added Malaysia and trimmed Turkey modestly while keeping Turkey first.
- NFL: Seattle beat San Francisco to clinch the NFC No. 1 seed, with Sam Darnold at quarterback. Raised Seattle in the Super Bowl market and Darnold in the NFC champion QB market.
- Morocco AFCON final penalty: Brahim Diaz scored his fourth goal of the tournament to put Morocco into the quarter-finals, with Hakimi assisting and El Kaabi missing chances. Moved Brahim above Hakimi for the missed stoppage-time penalty market.
- Tyson Fury comeback: Sky Sports reported Fury is returning and that Joshua's fatal crash likely delays Joshua's boxing plans; if Joshua is unavailable, Usyk trilogy or Fabio Wardley are plausible. Raised Usyk and Wardley while leaving Joshua first/competitive.
- Armada region: UK coverage described a major American force build-up in the waters off South America and an armada around Venezuela. Raised South America and added small Venezuela/Caribbean backups.

Watched but no update:
- Greenland meeting/person and venue still point most plausibly to Rubio/State Department despite the latest Trump-Greenland threats; no request-meeting report yet.
- Iran satellite-imagery site, largest immigration-enforcement state, Qatar Board of Peace, Mark Carney itinerary, NIDA poll, Sabalenka Brisbane final opponent, and Somalia severed agreements had no direct Jan. 4 evidence.
- Bangladesh's T20 World Cup dispute is now explicit, but the current reports emphasise moving Bangladesh's matches to Sri Lanka rather than replacing Bangladesh, so left the replacement-team market unchanged.

## 2026-01-05 Update

Resolution events:
- Syria Aleppo operation target resolved to SDF. Our Jan. 4 update was correct and benefited from treating failed SDF integration talks as the controlling signal.
- Iranian concrete-shield complex resolved to Parchin. The earlier satellite/site prior was correct.
- Denmark/Greenland requested-meeting person resolved to Marco Rubio. The State Department/Rubio channel was the right inference from the Greenland diplomatic escalation.
- DR Congo AFCON elimination resolved to Algeria. The bracket-first approach worked.
- British MPs World Cup host-exclusion country resolved to United States. Venezuela fallout and UK political criticism were relevant.
- Largest federal immigration-enforcement operation state resolved to Minnesota. We missed by staying on Illinois/California; lesson is to react harder when federal agencies surge resources into a named state, even if the trigger is fraud/immigration rather than a classic sanctuary-city operation.

Updates made:
- Minneapolis shooting-agency market: Jan. 5 CBS/Fox coverage said roughly 2,000 DHS agents were deploying to Minneapolis, specifically ICE deportation officers and Homeland Security Investigations. Raised ICE and added HSI/DHS while cutting CBP/FBI.
- Manchester United derby manager: Sky/Independent reported Ruben Amorim was sacked and Darren Fletcher would take interim charge. Replaced the high Amorim forecast with Fletcher first and Glasner/Southgate/Maresca/Carrick as backups.
- AFCON Morocco semifinal opponent: Nigeria beat Mozambique 4-0 and now awaits Algeria or DR Congo. Moved Nigeria just ahead of Algeria while keeping both high.
- Grok ban: EU, France, Ireland and the UK escalated formal scrutiny of Grok sexualized-image generation. Trimmed Turkey and raised France/UK, with Malaysia and India still live.
- Marc Guehi: Sky reported Manchester City's Gvardiol injury could accelerate a Guehi move, while Liverpool interest remains. Moved Manchester City ahead of Liverpool.
- Bangladesh cricket: Bangladesh refused to play T20 World Cup matches in India, asked the ICC to move them to Sri Lanka, and banned IPL broadcasts. Raised Bangladesh for Pakistan's potential boycott-support explanation and raised UAE/Oman as likely replacement-team names if relocation fails.
- Venezuela oil/armada: Jan. 5 coverage emphasized China's role as buyer/creditor and described massed U.S. air/naval power in the Caribbean. Raised China for the Massie oil-proceeds market and Caribbean for the armada-region market while keeping South America first.
- Iran protests: fresh coverage continued to concentrate early deaths and security-force casualties in Lorestan/Lur areas, with Chaharmahal and Bakhtiari also live. Raised Lorestan and trimmed Sistan.
- Sparse near-term markets: added modest top-five coverage for Carney after Beijing, NATO Greenland patrol mission name, diverted aircraft carrier, West Bank wedding raid, Kyiv embassy damage, Oreshnik target city, U.S. trade-deficit doubling, and Ukraine opposition bribery so low-coverage forecasts do not overly depend on one or two guesses.

Watched but no update:
- Qatar/Board of Peace still has no direct Jan. 5 article; Board of Peace remains the dominant prior.
- Gaza technocratic committee and leader questions had no new direct naming signal.
- Syria Maskana/Deir Hafer reinforcements had no direct organization naming beyond broader Syria/Israel talks and ISIL/Hezbollah background.

## 2026-01-06 Update

Resolution events:
- Minneapolis shooting-agency resolved to ICE. The Jan. 5 move toward ICE/HSI was correct; lesson is to treat named DHS deployments and ICE deportation-officer framing as stronger than generic DHS wording.
- Qatar peace body resolved to Board of Peace. The high Board of Peace prior was correct.

Updates made:
- Greenland talks venue: Greenland and Denmark asked for a quick meeting with Secretary of State Marco Rubio, but reports still did not name a venue. Raised State Department while keeping White House and other Washington venues as small backups.
- AFCON Morocco semifinal opponent: Algeria beat DR Congo in extra time and will face Nigeria in the quarterfinal. Removed DR Congo and concentrated probability on Nigeria and Algeria.
- Manchester United derby manager: Sky/Reuters reported Fletcher will handle Burnley and possibly the next match, while United are actively talking to Solskjaer and Carrick and want someone in place before the Manchester derby. Lowered Fletcher from a majority and added Solskjaer/Carrick.
- Marc Guehi: Manchester City injuries to Ruben Dias and Josko Gvardiol led Sky to report City may fast-track a defender move, bringing Guehi into contention. Raised Manchester City over Liverpool.
- Somalia agreements: Israel's foreign minister visited Somaliland after Israel recognized it, and Somalia condemned the visit as unlawful interference. Raised Israel for the possible agreement-severance market.
- Iran protests: state-linked and international coverage continued to point to Lorestan, especially Azna/Kouhdasht and security-force casualty claims. Raised Lorestan for the province market.
- Grok ban: Jan. 6 coverage showed widening UK, EU, German, French, Indian and Malaysian pressure but still no formal ban. Kept Turkey first, raised India modestly because New Delhi had issued a direct ultimatum to X/Grok.
- Senate funding-bill department: rereading the environmental-regulation wording suggested the omitted Department of the Interior is a better fit than DHS alone. Added Interior first.
- Syria/SDF: deadly Aleppo clashes and Syrian government accusations made SDF more likely for the eastern Aleppo fighters market, while Kobane remains the top city for the later anticipated-assault market with Aleppo added as a serious hedge.
- Venezuelan oil proceeds: Reuters-style coverage emphasized Chinese-bound Venezuelan crude and China as the top buyer, so raised China for the Massie oil-proceeds market.

Watched but no update:
- Mark Carney's post-Beijing itinerary still had no direct reporting.
- NATO's Greenland/Arctic mission name, U.S. carrier diversion, Oreshnik target city, Kyiv embassy damage, West Bank wedding raid, Thai NIDA polling, Gaza technocratic committee/leader, and the Southeast Asian visa-pause market still lacked direct Jan. 6 naming evidence.

## 2026-01-07 Update

Resolution events:
- Greenland talks venue resolved to White House. We over-weighted the State Department because Rubio was the named meeting target; lesson is that senior diplomacy with Trump/Greenland can still be staged at the White House even when the Secretary of State channel initiates it.
- Mark Carney post-Beijing stop resolved to Qatar. We missed by leaning on regional Asia/India itinerary logic without enough direct travel-calendar coverage.
- NATO Arctic mission name resolved to Arctic Sentry. The low-probability naming hedge worked; keep including exact mission-name guesses when wording suggests an official operation title.

Updates made:
- Manchester United derby manager: Irish Times reporting said Darren Fletcher's Burnley match was "first, and perhaps only" as interim, with talks continuing around Ole Gunnar Solskjaer and Michael Carrick. Moved Solskjaer and Carrick above Fletcher for the Jan. 17 derby.
- Syria/SDF: Al Jazeera reported Syria was closing Aleppo's Kurdish areas as military zones, warning civilians out, and preparing/launching an operation against SDF positions around Sheikh Maqsoud and Ashrafiyah. Raised Aleppo sharply for the anticipated assault city, raised SDF for eastern-Aleppo fighter questions, added Syrian Democratic Forces in full-form where the answer format requires it, and shifted the later complete-control city market toward Aleppo.
- Al-Shaddadi: the same failed SDF-government integration context and army offensive posture increased the chance of a Syrian army takeover, while SDF remains live as the incumbent/local force.
- Venezuela oil/armada: Jan. 7 Al Jazeera coverage said the United States would control Venezuelan oil sales, with proceeds settling first in US-controlled accounts, and also described tanker seizures in the Caribbean/North Atlantic. Moved the Massie oil-proceeds market to United States first and the "big armada" region market to Caribbean first.
- Bangladesh cricket: BCB and ICC were reported to be working through India's T20 World Cup security dispute, making an outright boycott-support framing less certain. Trimmed Bangladesh modestly but left it first.
- Portugal runoff: broad election coverage names Henrique Gouveia e Melo, Luis Marques Mendes, Antonio Jose Seguro, and Andre Ventura as the major field. Added Seguro and made the non-Ventura runoff spot more evenly split.
- Greenland framework person: after the White House venue miss and current leadership coverage, added Greenland PM Jens-Frederik Nielsen and removed stale Mute Egede exposure while keeping Mette Frederiksen first.
- Marc Guehi: Sky reported Oliver Glasner expects Guehi to stay at Palace in January even as Manchester City weighs accelerating a move. Kept City first but lowered it and raised Crystal Palace retention/no-deal probability.

Watched but no update:
- AFCON bracket now clearly has Morocco-Cameroon and Algeria-Nigeria quarterfinals, so Nigeria/Algeria remain the controlling options for Morocco's semifinal opponent.
- Yemen's Jan. 7 news centered on the STC/PLC crisis rather than a named new anti-Houthi military body, so left National Shield Forces first.
- Somalia/Israel news reinforced the Israel-Somaliland dispute but did not require changing the already high Israel forecasts.
- Thai NIDA polling, Ukraine embassy/Oreshnik/carrier questions, Gaza technocratic committee names, Senate funding department, Grok ban country, and sparse local incident markets had no stronger direct naming signal today.
